revision.comment: Bot: prepend Wikidata AFC comment via pages.txt
revision.text:
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11390433]] Wikidata name: Yatsugatake Mountains [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:30, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Volcanic mountain range in Japan}} {{Infobox mountain
| name= Yatsugatake Mountains | native_name={{native name|ja|八ヶ岳連峰}} | other_name= | etymology= Mountain with eight peaks<ref name="Hunt" />
| photo= Mount Yatsu.JPG | photo_caption= Yatsugatake Mountains, viewed from [[:en:Minamimaki, Nagano|Minamimaki, Nagano]].
| country= Japan | subdivision1_type= States | subdivision1= {{enum|Nagano Prefecture|Yamanashi Prefecture}} | subdivision2_type= Region | subdivision2= [[:en:Chūbu region|Chūbu]]
| highest= [[:en:Mount Aka (Yatsugatake)|Mount Aka]] | elevation_m= 2899 | coordinates=
| length_km= 30 | length_orientation= North-South | width_km= | width_orientation= | area_km2=
| geology= Volcanic | orogeny= Island arc | age= Quaternary | biome= Alpine climate
| map= | map_caption= }}
The {{nihongo|'''Yatsugatake Mountains'''|八ヶ岳連峰|Yatsugatake-renpō}} are a volcanic mountain range on the border between [[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]] and [[:en:Yamanashi Prefecture|Yamanashi Prefecture]] on the island of [[Honshu|Honshū]] in [[:en:Japan|Japan]].<ref name="Hunt" />
==Description== The mountain range consists of two volcanic groups, [[:en:Northern Yatsugatake Volcanic Group|Northern Yatsugatake Volcanic Group]] and [[:en:Southern Yatsugatake Volcanic Group|Southern Yatsugatake Volcanic Group]]. The two volcanic groups of Yatsugatake, North and South, have different characteristics. The North Yatsugatake mountains are gradual and lower while the South Yatsugatake mountains are steep and are more alpine in nature.
[[File:Yatsugatake Volcanic Chain 3D 2012 a.jpg|left|thumb|150px|Yatsugatake Mountains]] The southern group ranges from the [[Natsuzawa Pass]] to the end of the mountain range. The northern range includes [[:en:Mount Yoko (Northern Yatsugatake)|Mount Yoko]] to the other end of the range. [[:en:Mount Tateshina|Mount Tateshina]] is also included.
These mountains form a major part of the [[:en:Yatsugatake-Chūshin Kōgen Quasi-National Park|Yatsugatake-Chūshin Kōgen Quasi-National Park]].<ref name="MoEJ">{{cite web|url=http://www.biodic.go.jp/jpark/qnp/yatugata.html|script-title=ja:八ヶ岳中信高原国定公園|work=Japan Integrated Biodiversity Information System|publisher=Ministry of the Environment, Government of Japan|language=ja|access-date=2008-09-05}}</ref>
==Geology== These mountains consist of older eroded [[:en:Complex volcano|Complex volcano]]es, which were made by repeated volcanic eruptions over many thousands years. The last eruption was estimated from 600 to 800 years ago at Mount Yoko of North Yatsugatake Mountains. This is the only remaining active volcano.<ref name="QVoJ">{{cite web|url=http://riodb02.ibase.aist.go.jp/strata/VOL_JP/EN/vol/65.htm|title=KITA YATSU-GA-TAKE (TATESHINA-YAMA, YOKO-DAKE etc.)|year=2006|work=Quaternary Volcanoes of Japan|publisher=Geological Survey of Japan, AIST|access-date=2008-08-21}}</ref> The other volcanoes are extinct.<ref name="Hunt">{{cite book|last=Hunt|first=Paul|title=Hiking in Japan: An Adventurer's Guide to the Mountain Trails|publisher=Kodansha International Ltd.|location=Tokyo|year=1988|edition=First|pages=111–112|chapter=14. Eight Peaks: Yatsu-ga-take 八ヶ岳|isbn=0-87011-893-5}}</ref>
The volcanoes consist mostly of pyroxene andesite. Yatsugatake also lies nearby to the Fossa Magna ([[:ja:フォッサマグナ|ja]]).<ref name="Hunt" />
==Local legend== According to legend, Yatsugatake was once higher than [[:en:Mount Fuji|Mount Fuji]], but [[Konohanasakuya-hime]], the goddess of Mount Fuji, tore it down out of jealousy, leaving the collection of peaks that exists today. Paul Hunt muses that this might not be far from the truth considering that Yatsugatake is older than Fuji and as Fuji rose in prominence Yatsugatake wore away.<ref name="Hunt" />
==Ecology== Deciduous forests reach up 800-1700m above sea level. Above this zone is the [[:en:subalpine zone|subalpine zone]] reaching up to 2500m. [[:en:Siberian dwarf pine|Siberian dwarf pine]] inhabits this subalpine zone. On one part of Mount Nishi under 1700m grows a rare form of spruce, ''[[:en:Picea koyamae|Picea koyamae]]''. [[:en:Rock Ptarmigan|Rock Ptarmigan]] (''Lagopus muta'') are also native to these mountains, but are under threat due to loss of the Siberian dwarf pine habitat and also due to expansion of the range of the [[:en:Red fox|Red fox]]. Humans have enabled the expansion of the red fox by leaving trash behind in the mountains. Also growing in the subalpine zone are ''[[:en:Abies veitchii|Abies veitchii]]'' and ''[[:en:Abies mariesii|Abies mariesii]]''.
==Gallery== <gallery mode=packed style="font-size:90%"> File:高根町から見た八ヶ岳 - panoramio.jpg|Southern Yatsugatake Volcanic Group from the SSE Minami-Yatsugatake from Mt.Kitayokodake 04.jpg|Southern Yatsugatake from the NNW File:Tengudake7.JPG|Northern Yatsugatake Volcanic Group from the S File:Mt.Akadake from Mt.Yokodake 06.jpg|[[:en:Mount Aka (Yatsugatake)|Mount Aka]] File:Mt.Amidadake from Mt.Yokodake 10.jpg|Mount Amida File:Tateshinayama-fuyu.JPG|[[:en:Mount Tateshina|Mount Tateshina]] File:Yatsugatake-Kogen-Higashizawa-Bridge.jpg|Mt. Yatsugatake and Higashizawa Bridge [[:en:Hokuto, Yamanashi|Hokuto]], [[:en:Yamanashi Prefecture|Yamanashi]] </gallery>
==References== {{reflist}}
== External links == {{commons Category|Yatsugatake Mountains}} * [https://gbank.gsj.jp/volcano/Quat_Vol/volcano_data/E84.html Kita Yatsugatake (Northern Yatsugatake)] - Geological Survey of Japan * [https://gbank.gsj.jp/volcano/Quat_Vol/gallery/E85.htm Minami Yatsugatake (Southern Yatsugatake)] - Geological Survey of Japan * {{cite gvp|name=Yatsugatake|vn=283824|access-date=2021-06-24}}
{{coord|35.9708|N|138.3700|E|source:wikidata|display=title}}
‎]]

| length_km= 30 | length_orientation= North-South | width_km= | width_orientation= | area_km2=
| geology= Volcanic | orogeny= Island arc | age= Quaternary | biome= Alpine climate
| map= | map_caption= }}
The {{nihongo|'''Yatsugatake Mountains'''|八ヶ岳連峰|Yatsugatake-renpō}} are a volcanic mountain range on the border between [[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]] and [[:en:Yamanashi Prefecture|Yamanashi Prefecture]] on the island of [[Honshu|Honshū]] in [[:en:Japan|Japan]].<ref name="Hunt" />
==Description== The mountain range consists of two volcanic groups, [[:en:Northern Yatsugatake Volcanic Group|Northern Yatsugatake Volcanic Group]] and [[:en:Southern Yatsugatake Volcanic Group|Southern Yatsugatake Volcanic Group]]. The two volcanic groups of Yatsugatake, North and South, have different characteristics. The North Yatsugatake mountains are gradual and lower while the South Yatsugatake mountains are steep and are more alpine in nature.
[[File:Yatsugatake Volcanic Chain 3D 2012 a.jpg|left|thumb|150px|Yatsugatake Mountains]] The southern group ranges from the [[Natsuzawa Pass]] to the end of the mountain range. The northern range includes [[:en:Mount Yoko (Northern Yatsugatake)|Mount Yoko]] to the other end of the range. [[:en:Mount Tateshina|Mount Tateshina]] is also included.
These mountains form a major part of the [[:en:Yatsugatake-Chūshin Kōgen Quasi-National Park|Yatsugatake-Chūshin Kōgen Quasi-National Park]].<ref name="MoEJ">{{cite web|url=http://www.biodic.go.jp/jpark/qnp/yatugata.html|script-title=ja:八ヶ岳中信高原国定公園|work=Japan Integrated Biodiversity Information System|publisher=Ministry of the Environment, Government of Japan|language=ja|access-date=2008-09-05}}</ref>
==Geology== These mountains consist of older eroded [[:en:Complex volcano|Complex volcano]]es, which were made by repeated volcanic eruptions over many thousands years. The last eruption was estimated from 600 to 800 years ago at Mount Yoko of North Yatsugatake Mountains. This is the only remaining active volcano.<ref name="QVoJ">{{cite web|url=http://riodb02.ibase.aist.go.jp/strata/VOL_JP/EN/vol/65.htm|title=KITA YATSU-GA-TAKE (TATESHINA-YAMA, YOKO-DAKE etc.)|year=2006|work=Quaternary Volcanoes of Japan|publisher=Geological Survey of Japan, AIST|access-date=2008-08-21}}</ref> The other volcanoes are extinct.<ref name="Hunt">{{cite book|last=Hunt|first=Paul|title=Hiking in Japan: An Adventurer's Guide to the Mountain Trails|publisher=Kodansha International Ltd.|location=Tokyo|year=1988|edition=First|pages=111–112|chapter=14. Eight Peaks: Yatsu-ga-take 八ヶ岳|isbn=0-87011-893-5}}</ref>
The volcanoes consist mostly of pyroxene andesite. Yatsugatake also lies nearby to the Fossa Magna ([[:ja:フォッサマグナ|ja]]).<ref name="Hunt" />
==Local legend== According to legend, Yatsugatake was once higher than [[:en:Mount Fuji|Mount Fuji]], but [[Konohanasakuya-hime]], the goddess of Mount Fuji, tore it down out of jealousy, leaving the collection of peaks that exists today. Paul Hunt muses that this might not be far from the truth considering that Yatsugatake is older than Fuji and as Fuji rose in prominence Yatsugatake wore away.<ref name="Hunt" />
==Ecology== Deciduous forests reach up 800-1700m above sea level. Above this zone is the [[:en:subalpine zone|subalpine zone]] reaching up to 2500m. [[:en:Siberian dwarf pine|Siberian dwarf pine]] inhabits this subalpine zone. On one part of Mount Nishi under 1700m grows a rare form of spruce, ''[[:en:Picea koyamae|Picea koyamae]]''. [[:en:Rock Ptarmigan|Rock Ptarmigan]] (''Lagopus muta'') are also native to these mountains, but are under threat due to loss of the Siberian dwarf pine habitat and also due to expansion of the range of the [[:en:Red fox|Red fox]]. Humans have enabled the expansion of the red fox by leaving trash behind in the mountains. Also growing in the subalpine zone are ''[[:en:Abies veitchii|Abies veitchii]]'' and ''[[:en:Abies mariesii|Abies mariesii]]''.
==Gallery== <gallery mode=packed style="font-size:90%"> File:高根町から見た八ヶ岳 - panoramio.jpg|Southern Yatsugatake Volcanic Group from the SSE Minami-Yatsugatake from Mt.Kitayokodake 04.jpg|Southern Yatsugatake from the NNW File:Tengudake7.JPG|Northern Yatsugatake Volcanic Group from the S File:Mt.Akadake from Mt.Yokodake 06.jpg|[[:en:Mount Aka (Yatsugatake)|Mount Aka]] File:Mt.Amidadake from Mt.Yokodake 10.jpg|Mount Amida File:Tateshinayama-fuyu.JPG|[[:en:Mount Tateshina|Mount Tateshina]] File:Yatsugatake-Kogen-Higashizawa-Bridge.jpg|Mt. Yatsugatake and Higashizawa Bridge [[:en:Hokuto, Yamanashi|Hokuto]], [[:en:Yamanashi Prefecture|Yamanashi]] </gallery>

Eight Peaks: Yatsu-ga-take 八ヶ岳|isbn=0-87011-893-5}}</ref>
The volcanoes consist mostly of pyroxene andesite. Yatsugatake also lies nearby to the Fossa Magna ([[:ja:フォッサマグナ|ja]]).<ref name="Hunt" />
==Local legend== According to legend, Yatsugatake was once higher than [[:en:Mount Fuji|Mount Fuji]], but [[Konohanasakuya-hime]], the goddess of Mount Fuji, tore it down out of jealousy, leaving the collection of peaks that exists today. Paul Hunt muses that this might not be far from the truth considering that Yatsugatake is older than Fuji and as Fuji rose in prominence Yatsugatake wore away.<ref name="Hunt" />
==Ecology== Deciduous forests reach up 800-1700m above sea level. Above this zone is the [[:en:subalpine zone|subalpine zone]] reaching up to 2500m. [[:en:Siberian dwarf pine|Siberian dwarf pine]] inhabits this subalpine zone. On one part of Mount Nishi under 1700m grows a rare form of spruce, ''[[:en:Picea koyamae|Picea koyamae]]''. [[:en:Rock Ptarmigan|Rock Ptarmigan]] (''Lagopus muta'') are also native to these mountains, but are under threat due to loss of the Siberian dwarf pine habitat and also due to expansion of the range of the [[:en:Red fox|Red fox]]. Humans have enabled the expansion of the red fox by leaving trash behind in the mountains. Also growing in the subalpine zone are ''[[:en:Abies veitchii|Abies veitchii]]'' and ''[[:en:Abies mariesii|Abies mariesii]]''.

| country= Japan | subdivision1_type= States | subdivision1= {{enum|Nagano Prefecture|Yamanashi Prefecture}} | subdivision2_type= Region | subdivision2= [[:en:Chūbu region|Chūbu]]

The {{nihongo|'''Yatsugatake Mountains'''|八ヶ岳連峰|Yatsugatake-renpō}} are a volcanic mountain range on the border between [[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]] and [[:en:Yamanashi Prefecture|Yamanashi Prefecture]] on the island of [[Honshu|Honshū]] in [[:en:Japan|Japan]].<ref name="Hunt" />
==Description== The mountain range consists of two volcanic groups, [[:en:Northern Yatsugatake Volcanic Group|Northern Yatsugatake Volcanic Group]] and [[:en:Southern Yatsugatake Volcanic Group|Southern Yatsugatake Volcanic Group]]. The two volcanic groups of Yatsugatake, North and South, have different characteristics. The North Yatsugatake mountains are gradual and lower while the South Yatsugatake mountains are steep and are more alpine in nature.
[[File:Yatsugatake Volcanic Chain 3D 2012 a.jpg|left|thumb|150px|Yatsugatake Mountains]] The southern group ranges from the [[Natsuzawa Pass]] to the end of the mountain range. The northern range includes [[:en:Mount Yoko (Northern Yatsugatake)|Mount Yoko]] to the other end of the range. [[:en:Mount Tateshina|Mount Tateshina]] is also included.
These mountains form a major part of the [[:en:Yatsugatake-Chūshin Kōgen Quasi-National Park|Yatsugatake-Chūshin Kōgen Quasi-National Park]].<ref name="MoEJ">{{cite web|url=http://www.biodic.go.jp/jpark/qnp/yatugata.html|script-title=ja:八ヶ岳中信高原国定公園|work=Japan Integrated Biodiversity Information System|publisher=Ministry of the Environment, Government of Japan|language=ja|access-date=2008-09-05}}</ref>
==Geology== These mountains consist of older eroded [[:en:Complex volcano|Complex volcano]]es, which were made by repeated volcanic eruptions over many thousands years. The last eruption was estimated from 600 to 800 years ago at Mount Yoko of North Yatsugatake Mountains. This is the only remaining active volcano.<ref name="QVoJ">{{cite web|url=http://riodb02.ibase.aist.go.jp/strata/VOL_JP/EN/vol/65.htm|title=KITA YATSU-GA-TAKE (TATESHINA-YAMA, YOKO-DAKE etc.)|year=2006|work=Quaternary Volcanoes of Japan|publisher=Geological Survey of Japan, AIST|access-date=2008-08-21}}</ref> The other volcanoes are extinct.<ref name="Hunt">{{cite book|last=Hunt|first=Paul|title=Hiking in Japan: An Adventurer's Guide to the Mountain Trails|publisher=Kodansha International Ltd.|location=Tokyo|year=1988|edition=First|pages=111–112|chapter=14. Eight Peaks: Yatsu-ga-take 八ヶ岳|isbn=0-87011-893-5}}</ref>
The volcanoes consist mostly of pyroxene andesite. Yatsugatake also lies nearby to the Fossa Magna ([[:ja:フォッサマグナ|ja]]).<ref name="Hunt" />
==Local legend== According to legend, Yatsugatake was once higher than [[:en:Mount Fuji|Mount Fuji]], but [[Konohanasakuya-hime]], the goddess of Mount Fuji, tore it down out of jealousy, leaving the collection of peaks that exists today. Paul Hunt muses that this might not be far from the truth considering that Yatsugatake is older than Fuji and as Fuji rose in prominence Yatsugatake wore away.<ref name="Hunt" />
==Ecology== Deciduous forests reach up 800-1700m above sea level. Above this zone is the [[:en:subalpine zone|subalpine zone]] reaching up to 2500m. [[:en:Siberian dwarf pine|Siberian dwarf pine]] inhabits this subalpine zone. On one part of Mount Nishi under 1700m grows a rare form of spruce, ''[[:en:Picea koyamae|Picea koyamae]]''. [[:en:Rock Ptarmigan|Rock Ptarmigan]] (''Lagopus muta'') are also native to these mountains, but are under threat due to loss of the Siberian dwarf pine habitat and also due to expansion of the range of the [[:en:Red fox|Red fox]]. Humans have enabled the expansion of the red fox by leaving trash behind in the mountains. Also growing in the subalpine zone are ''[[:en:Abies veitchii|Abies veitchii]]'' and ''[[:en:Abies mariesii|Abies mariesii]]''.

Yatsugatake and Higashizawa Bridge [[:en:Hokuto, Yamanashi|Hokuto]], [[:en:Yamanashi Prefecture|Yamanashi]] </gallery>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[arz:جبال ياتسوجاتاكى]][[en:Yatsugatake Mountains]][[fa:کوه یاتسوداتاکه]][[fr:Monts Yatsugatake]][[ja:八ヶ岳連峰]][[lld:Ciadëina de Yatsugatake]][[sco:Yatsugatake Bens]][[zh:八岳連峰]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11390433]] Wikidata name: Yatsugatake Mountains [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:30, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Volcanic mountain range in Japan}} {{Infobox mountain
| name= Yatsugatake Mountains | native_name={{native name|ja|八ヶ岳連峰}} | other_name= | etymology= Mountain with eight peaks<ref name="Hunt" />
| photo= Mount Yatsu.JPG | photo_caption= Yatsugatake Mountains, viewed from [[:en:Minamimaki, Nagano|Minamimaki, Nagano]].
| country= Japan | subdivision1_type= States | subdivision1= {{enum|Nagano Prefecture|Yamanashi Prefecture}} | subdivision2_type= Region | subdivision2= [[:en:Chūbu region|Chūbu]]
| highest= [[:en:Mount Aka (Yatsugatake)|Mount Aka]] | elevation_m= 2899 | coordinates=
| length_km= 30 | length_orientation= North-South | width_km= | width_orientation= | area_km2=
| geology= Volcanic | orogeny= Island arc | age= Quaternary | biome= Alpine climate
| map= | map_caption= }}
The {{nihongo|'''Yatsugatake Mountains'''|八ヶ岳連峰|Yatsugatake-renpō}} are a volcanic mountain range on the border between [[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]] and [[:en:Yamanashi Prefecture|Yamanashi Prefecture]] on the island of [[Honshu|Honshū]] in [[:en:Japan|Japan]].<ref name="Hunt" />
==Description== The mountain range consists of two volcanic groups, [[:en:Northern Yatsugatake Volcanic Group|Northern Yatsugatake Volcanic Group]] and [[:en:Southern Yatsugatake Volcanic Group|Southern Yatsugatake Volcanic Group]]. The two volcanic groups of Yatsugatake, North and South, have different characteristics. The North Yatsugatake mountains are gradual and lower while the South Yatsugatake mountains are steep and are more alpine in nature.
[[File:Yatsugatake Volcanic Chain 3D 2012 a.jpg|left|thumb|150px|Yatsugatake Mountains]] The southern group ranges from the [[Natsuzawa Pass]] to the end of the mountain range. The northern range includes [[:en:Mount Yoko (Northern Yatsugatake)|Mount Yoko]] to the other end of the range. [[:en:Mount Tateshina|Mount Tateshina]] is also included.
These mountains form a major part of the [[:en:Yatsugatake-Chūshin Kōgen Quasi-National Park|Yatsugatake-Chūshin Kōgen Quasi-National Park]].<ref name="MoEJ">{{cite web|url=http://www.biodic.go.jp/jpark/qnp/yatugata.html|script-title=ja:八ヶ岳中信高原国定公園|work=Japan Integrated Biodiversity Information System|publisher=Ministry of the Environment, Government of Japan|language=ja|access-date=2008-09-05}}</ref>
==Geology== These mountains consist of older eroded [[:en:Complex volcano|Complex volcano]]es, which were made by repeated volcanic eruptions over many thousands years. The last eruption was estimated from 600 to 800 years ago at Mount Yoko of North Yatsugatake Mountains. This is the only remaining active volcano.<ref name="QVoJ">{{cite web|url=http://riodb02.ibase.aist.go.jp/strata/VOL_JP/EN/vol/65.htm|title=KITA YATSU-GA-TAKE (TATESHINA-YAMA, YOKO-DAKE etc.)|year=2006|work=Quaternary Volcanoes of Japan|publisher=Geological Survey of Japan, AIST|access-date=2008-08-21}}</ref> The other volcanoes are extinct.<ref name="Hunt">{{cite book|last=Hunt|first=Paul|title=Hiking in Japan: An Adventurer's Guide to the Mountain Trails|publisher=Kodansha International Ltd.|location=Tokyo|year=1988|edition=First|pages=111–112|chapter=14. Eight Peaks: Yatsu-ga-take 八ヶ岳|isbn=0-87011-893-5}}</ref>
The volcanoes consist mostly of pyroxene andesite. Yatsugatake also lies nearby to the Fossa Magna ([[:ja:フォッサマグナ|ja]]).<ref name="Hunt" />
==Local legend== According to legend, Yatsugatake was once higher than [[:en:Mount Fuji|Mount Fuji]], but [[Konohanasakuya-hime]], the goddess of Mount Fuji, tore it down out of jealousy, leaving the collection of peaks that exists today. Paul Hunt muses that this might not be far from the truth considering that Yatsugatake is older than Fuji and as Fuji rose in prominence Yatsugatake wore away.<ref name="Hunt" />
==Ecology== Deciduous forests reach up 800-1700m above sea level. Above this zone is the [[:en:subalpine zone|subalpine zone]] reaching up to 2500m. [[:en:Siberian dwarf pine|Siberian dwarf pine]] inhabits this subalpine zone. On one part of Mount Nishi under 1700m grows a rare form of spruce, ''[[:en:Picea koyamae|Picea koyamae]]''. [[:en:Rock Ptarmigan|Rock Ptarmigan]] (''Lagopus muta'') are also native to these mountains, but are under threat due to loss of the Siberian dwarf pine habitat and also due to expansion of the range of the [[:en:Red fox|Red fox]]. Humans have enabled the expansion of the red fox by leaving trash behind in the mountains. Also growing in the subalpine zone are ''[[:en:Abies veitchii|Abies veitchii]]'' and ''[[:en:Abies mariesii|Abies mariesii]]''.
==Gallery== <gallery mode=packed style="font-size:90%"> File:高根町から見た八ヶ岳 - panoramio.jpg|Southern Yatsugatake Volcanic Group from the SSE Minami-Yatsugatake from Mt.Kitayokodake 04.jpg|Southern Yatsugatake from the NNW File:Tengudake7.JPG|Northern Yatsugatake Volcanic Group from the S File:Mt.Akadake from Mt.Yokodake 06.jpg|[[:en:Mount Aka (Yatsugatake)|Mount Aka]] File:Mt.Amidadake from Mt.Yokodake 10.jpg|Mount Amida File:Tateshinayama-fuyu.JPG|[[:en:Mount Tateshina|Mount Tateshina]] File:Yatsugatake-Kogen-Higashizawa-Bridge.jpg|Mt. Yatsugatake and Higashizawa Bridge [[:en:Hokuto, Yamanashi|Hokuto]], [[:en:Yamanashi Prefecture|Yamanashi]] </gallery>

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[arz:جبال ياتسوجاتاكى]][[en:Yatsugatake Mountains]][[fa:کوه یاتسوداتاکه]][[fr:Monts Yatsugatake]][[ja:八ヶ岳連峰]][[lld:Ciadëina de Yatsugatake]][[sco:Yatsugatake Bens]][[zh:八岳連峰]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11390433]] Wikidata name: Yatsugatake Mountains [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:30, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Volcanic mountain range in Japan}} {{Infobox mountain
| name= Yatsugatake Mountains | native_name={{native name|ja|八ヶ岳連峰}} | other_name= | etymology= Mountain with eight peaks<ref name="Hunt" />
| photo= Mount Yatsu.JPG | photo_caption= Yatsugatake Mountains, viewed from [[:en:Minamimaki, Nagano|Minamimaki, Nagano]].
| country= Japan | subdivision1_type= States | subdivision1= {{enum|Nagano Prefecture|Yamanashi Prefecture}} | subdivision2_type= Region | subdivision2= [[:en:Chūbu region|Chūbu]]
| highest= [[:en:Mount Aka (Yatsugatake)|Mount Aka]] | elevation_m= 2899 | coordinates=
| length_km= 30 | length_orientation= North-South | width_km= | width_orientation= | area_km2=
| geology= Volcanic | orogeny= Island arc | age= Quaternary | biome= Alpine climate
| map= | map_caption= }}
The {{nihongo|'''Yatsugatake Mountains'''|八ヶ岳連峰|Yatsugatake-renpō}} are a volcanic mountain range on the border between [[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]] and [[:en:Yamanashi Prefecture|Yamanashi Prefecture]] on the island of [[Honshu|Honshū]] in [[:en:Japan|Japan]].<ref name="Hunt" />
==Description== The mountain range consists of two volcanic groups, [[:en:Northern Yatsugatake Volcanic Group|Northern Yatsugatake Volcanic Group]] and [[:en:Southern Yatsugatake Volcanic Group|Southern Yatsugatake Volcanic Group]]. The two volcanic groups of Yatsugatake, North and South, have different characteristics. The North Yatsugatake mountains are gradual and lower while the South Yatsugatake mountains are steep and are more alpine in nature.
[[File:Yatsugatake Volcanic Chain 3D 2012 a.jpg|left|thumb|150px|Yatsugatake Mountains]] The southern group ranges from the [[Natsuzawa Pass]] to the end of the mountain range. The northern range includes [[:en:Mount Yoko (Northern Yatsugatake)|Mount Yoko]] to the other end of the range. [[:en:Mount Tateshina|Mount Tateshina]] is also included.
These mountains form a major part of the [[:en:Yatsugatake-Chūshin Kōgen Quasi-National Park|Yatsugatake-Chūshin Kōgen Quasi-National Park]].<ref name="MoEJ">{{cite web|url=http://www.biodic.go.jp/jpark/qnp/yatugata.html|script-title=ja:八ヶ岳中信高原国定公園|work=Japan Integrated Biodiversity Information System|publisher=Ministry of the Environment, Government of Japan|language=ja|access-date=2008-09-05}}</ref>
==Geology== These mountains consist of older eroded [[:en:Complex volcano|Complex volcano]]es, which were made by repeated volcanic eruptions over many thousands years. The last eruption was estimated from 600 to 800 years ago at Mount Yoko of North Yatsugatake Mountains. This is the only remaining active volcano.<ref name="QVoJ">{{cite web|url=http://riodb02.ibase.aist.go.jp/strata/VOL_JP/EN/vol/65.htm|title=KITA YATSU-GA-TAKE (TATESHINA-YAMA, YOKO-DAKE etc.)|year=2006|work=Quaternary Volcanoes of Japan|publisher=Geological Survey of Japan, AIST|access-date=2008-08-21}}</ref> The other volcanoes are extinct.<ref name="Hunt">{{cite book|last=Hunt|first=Paul|title=Hiking in Japan: An Adventurer's Guide to the Mountain Trails|publisher=Kodansha International Ltd.|location=Tokyo|year=1988|edition=First|pages=111–112|chapter=14. Eight Peaks: Yatsu-ga-take 八ヶ岳|isbn=0-87011-893-5}}</ref>
The volcanoes consist mostly of pyroxene andesite. Yatsugatake also lies nearby to the Fossa Magna ([[:ja:フォッサマグナ|ja]]).<ref name="Hunt" />
==Local legend== According to legend, Yatsugatake was once higher than [[:en:Mount Fuji|Mount Fuji]], but [[Konohanasakuya-hime]], the goddess of Mount Fuji, tore it down out of jealousy, leaving the collection of peaks that exists today. Paul Hunt muses that this might not be far from the truth considering that Yatsugatake is older than Fuji and as Fuji rose in prominence Yatsugatake wore away.<ref name="Hunt" />
==Ecology== Deciduous forests reach up 800-1700m above sea level. Above this zone is the [[:en:subalpine zone|subalpine zone]] reaching up to 2500m. [[:en:Siberian dwarf pine|Siberian dwarf pine]] inhabits this subalpine zone. On one part of Mount Nishi under 1700m grows a rare form of spruce, ''[[:en:Picea koyamae|Picea koyamae]]''. [[:en:Rock Ptarmigan|Rock Ptarmigan]] (''Lagopus muta'') are also native to these mountains, but are under threat due to loss of the Siberian dwarf pine habitat and also due to expansion of the range of the [[:en:Red fox|Red fox]]. Humans have enabled the expansion of the red fox by leaving trash behind in the mountains. Also growing in the subalpine zone are ''[[:en:Abies veitchii|Abies veitchii]]'' and ''[[:en:Abies mariesii|Abies mariesii]]''.
==Gallery== <gallery mode=packed style="font-size:90%"> File:高根町から見た八ヶ岳 - panoramio.jpg|Southern Yatsugatake Volcanic Group from the SSE Minami-Yatsugatake from Mt.Kitayokodake 04.jpg|Southern Yatsugatake from the NNW File:Tengudake7.JPG|Northern Yatsugatake Volcanic Group from the S File:Mt.Akadake from Mt.Yokodake 06.jpg|[[:en:Mount Aka (Yatsugatake)|Mount Aka]] File:Mt.Amidadake from Mt.Yokodake 10.jpg|Mount Amida File:Tateshinayama-fuyu.JPG|[[:en:Mount Tateshina|Mount Tateshina]] File:Yatsugatake-Kogen-Higashizawa-Bridge.jpg|Mt. Yatsugatake and Higashizawa Bridge [[:en:Hokuto, Yamanashi|Hokuto]], [[:en:Yamanashi Prefecture|Yamanashi]] </gallery>
==References== {{reflist}}
== External links == {{commons Category|Yatsugatake Mountains}} * [https://gbank.gsj.jp/volcano/Quat_Vol/volcano_data/E84.html Kita Yatsugatake (Northern Yatsugatake)] - Geological Survey of Japan * [https://gbank.gsj.jp/volcano/Quat_Vol/gallery/E85.htm Minami Yatsugatake (Southern Yatsugatake)] - Geological Survey of Japan * {{cite gvp|name=Yatsugatake|vn=283824|access-date=2021-06-24}}
{{coord|35.9708|N|138.3700|E|source:wikidata|display=title}}
‎]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[arz:جبال ياتسوجاتاكى]][[en:Yatsugatake Mountains]][[fa:کوه یاتسوداتاکه]][[fr:Monts Yatsugatake]][[ja:八ヶ岳連峰]][[lld:Ciadëina de Yatsugatake]][[sco:Yatsugatake Bens]][[zh:八岳連峰]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11390433]] Wikidata name: Yatsugatake Mountains [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:30, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Volcanic mountain range in Japan}} {{Infobox mountain
| name= Yatsugatake Mountains | native_name={{native name|ja|八ヶ岳連峰}} | other_name= | etymology= Mountain with eight peaks<ref name="Hunt" />
| photo= Mount Yatsu.JPG | photo_caption= Yatsugatake Mountains, viewed from [[:en:Minamimaki, Nagano|Minamimaki, Nagano]].
| country= Japan | subdivision1_type= States | subdivision1= {{enum|Nagano Prefecture|Yamanashi Prefecture}} | subdivision2_type= Region | subdivision2= [[:en:Chūbu region|Chūbu]]
| highest= [[:en:Mount Aka (Yatsugatake)|Mount Aka]] | elevation_m= 2899 | coordinates=
| length_km= 30 | length_orientation= North-South | width_km= | width_orientation= | area_km2=
| geology= Volcanic | orogeny= Island arc | age= Quaternary | biome= Alpine climate
| map= | map_caption= }}
The {{nihongo|'''Yatsugatake Mountains'''|八ヶ岳連峰|Yatsugatake-renpō}} are a volcanic mountain range on the border between [[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]] and [[:en:Yamanashi Prefecture|Yamanashi Prefecture]] on the island of [[Honshu|Honshū]] in [[:en:Japan|Japan]].<ref name="Hunt" />
==Description== The mountain range consists of two volcanic groups, [[:en:Northern Yatsugatake Volcanic Group|Northern Yatsugatake Volcanic Group]] and [[:en:Southern Yatsugatake Volcanic Group|Southern Yatsugatake Volcanic Group]]. The two volcanic groups of Yatsugatake, North and South, have different characteristics. The North Yatsugatake mountains are gradual and lower while the South Yatsugatake mountains are steep and are more alpine in nature.
[[File:Yatsugatake Volcanic Chain 3D 2012 a.jpg|left|thumb|150px|Yatsugatake Mountains]] The southern group ranges from the [[Natsuzawa Pass]] to the end of the mountain range. The northern range includes [[:en:Mount Yoko (Northern Yatsugatake)|Mount Yoko]] to the other end of the range. [[:en:Mount Tateshina|Mount Tateshina]] is also included.
These mountains form a major part of the [[:en:Yatsugatake-Chūshin Kōgen Quasi-National Park|Yatsugatake-Chūshin Kōgen Quasi-National Park]].<ref name="MoEJ">{{cite web|url=http://www.biodic.go.jp/jpark/qnp/yatugata.html|script-title=ja:八ヶ岳中信高原国定公園|work=Japan Integrated Biodiversity Information System|publisher=Ministry of the Environment, Government of Japan|language=ja|access-date=2008-09-05}}</ref>
==Geology== These mountains consist of older eroded [[:en:Complex volcano|Complex volcano]]es, which were made by repeated volcanic eruptions over many thousands years. The last eruption was estimated from 600 to 800 years ago at Mount Yoko of North Yatsugatake Mountains. This is the only remaining active volcano.<ref name="QVoJ">{{cite web|url=http://riodb02.ibase.aist.go.jp/strata/VOL_JP/EN/vol/65.htm|title=KITA YATSU-GA-TAKE (TATESHINA-YAMA, YOKO-DAKE etc.)|year=2006|work=Quaternary Volcanoes of Japan|publisher=Geological Survey of Japan, AIST|access-date=2008-08-21}}</ref> The other volcanoes are extinct.<ref name="Hunt">{{cite book|last=Hunt|first=Paul|title=Hiking in Japan: An Adventurer's Guide to the Mountain Trails|publisher=Kodansha International Ltd.|location=Tokyo|year=1988|edition=First|pages=111–112|chapter=14. Eight Peaks: Yatsu-ga-take 八ヶ岳|isbn=0-87011-893-5}}</ref>
The volcanoes consist mostly of pyroxene andesite. Yatsugatake also lies nearby to the Fossa Magna ([[:ja:フォッサマグナ|ja]]).<ref name="Hunt" />
==Local legend== According to legend, Yatsugatake was once higher than [[:en:Mount Fuji|Mount Fuji]], but [[Konohanasakuya-hime]], the goddess of Mount Fuji, tore it down out of jealousy, leaving the collection of peaks that exists today. Paul Hunt muses that this might not be far from the truth considering that Yatsugatake is older than Fuji and as Fuji rose in prominence Yatsugatake wore away.<ref name="Hunt" />
==Ecology== Deciduous forests reach up 800-1700m above sea level. Above this zone is the [[:en:subalpine zone|subalpine zone]] reaching up to 2500m. [[:en:Siberian dwarf pine|Siberian dwarf pine]] inhabits this subalpine zone. On one part of Mount Nishi under 1700m grows a rare form of spruce, ''[[:en:Picea koyamae|Picea koyamae]]''. [[:en:Rock Ptarmigan|Rock Ptarmigan]] (''Lagopus muta'') are also native to these mountains, but are under threat due to loss of the Siberian dwarf pine habitat and also due to expansion of the range of the [[:en:Red fox|Red fox]]. Humans have enabled the expansion of the red fox by leaving trash behind in the mountains. Also growing in the subalpine zone are ''[[:en:Abies veitchii|Abies veitchii]]'' and ''[[:en:Abies mariesii|Abies mariesii]]''.
==Gallery== <gallery mode=packed style="font-size:90%"> File:高根町から見た八ヶ岳 - panoramio.jpg|Southern Yatsugatake Volcanic Group from the SSE Minami-Yatsugatake from Mt.Kitayokodake 04.jpg|Southern Yatsugatake from the NNW File:Tengudake7.JPG|Northern Yatsugatake Volcanic Group from the S File:Mt.Akadake from Mt.Yokodake 06.jpg|[[:en:Mount Aka (Yatsugatake)|Mount Aka]] File:Mt.Amidadake from Mt.Yokodake 10.jpg|Mount Amida File:Tateshinayama-fuyu.JPG|[[:en:Mount Tateshina|Mount Tateshina]] File:Yatsugatake-Kogen-Higashizawa-Bridge.jpg|Mt. Yatsugatake and Higashizawa Bridge [[:en:Hokuto, Yamanashi|Hokuto]], [[:en:Yamanashi Prefecture|Yamanashi]] </gallery>
==References== {{reflist}}
== External links == {{commons Category|Yatsugatake Mountains}} * [https://gbank.gsj.jp/volcano/Quat_Vol/volcano_data/E84.html Kita Yatsugatake (Northern Yatsugatake)] - Geological Survey of Japan * [https://gbank.gsj.jp/volcano/Quat_Vol/gallery/E85.htm Minami Yatsugatake (Southern Yatsugatake)] - Geological Survey of Japan * {{cite gvp|name=Yatsugatake|vn=283824|access-date=2021-06-24}}
{{coord|35.9708|N|138.3700|E|source:wikidata|display=title}}
‎]]

The {{nihongo|'''Yatsugatake Mountains'''|八ヶ岳連峰|Yatsugatake-renpō}} are a volcanic mountain range on the border between [[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]] and [[:en:Yamanashi Prefecture|Yamanashi Prefecture]] on the island of [[Honshu|Honshū]] in [[:en:Japan|Japan]].<ref name="Hunt" />
==Description== The mountain range consists of two volcanic groups, [[:en:Northern Yatsugatake Volcanic Group|Northern Yatsugatake Volcanic Group]] and [[:en:Southern Yatsugatake Volcanic Group|Southern Yatsugatake Volcanic Group]]. The two volcanic groups of Yatsugatake, North and South, have different characteristics. The North Yatsugatake mountains are gradual and lower while the South Yatsugatake mountains are steep and are more alpine in nature.
[[File:Yatsugatake Volcanic Chain 3D 2012 a.jpg|left|thumb|150px|Yatsugatake Mountains]] The southern group ranges from the [[Natsuzawa Pass]] to the end of the mountain range. The northern range includes [[:en:Mount Yoko (Northern Yatsugatake)|Mount Yoko]] to the other end of the range. [[:en:Mount Tateshina|Mount Tateshina]] is also included.
These mountains form a major part of the [[:en:Yatsugatake-Chūshin Kōgen Quasi-National Park|Yatsugatake-Chūshin Kōgen Quasi-National Park]].<ref name="MoEJ">{{cite web|url=http://www.biodic.go.jp/jpark/qnp/yatugata.html|script-title=ja:八ヶ岳中信高原国定公園|work=Japan Integrated Biodiversity Information System|publisher=Ministry of the Environment, Government of Japan|language=ja|access-date=2008-09-05}}</ref>
==Geology== These mountains consist of older eroded [[:en:Complex volcano|Complex volcano]]es, which were made by repeated volcanic eruptions over many thousands years. The last eruption was estimated from 600 to 800 years ago at Mount Yoko of North Yatsugatake Mountains. This is the only remaining active volcano.<ref name="QVoJ">{{cite web|url=http://riodb02.ibase.aist.go.jp/strata/VOL_JP/EN/vol/65.htm|title=KITA YATSU-GA-TAKE (TATESHINA-YAMA, YOKO-DAKE etc.)|year=2006|work=Quaternary Volcanoes of Japan|publisher=Geological Survey of Japan, AIST|access-date=2008-08-21}}</ref> The other volcanoes are extinct.<ref name="Hunt">{{cite book|last=Hunt|first=Paul|title=Hiking in Japan: An Adventurer's Guide to the Mountain Trails|publisher=Kodansha International Ltd.|location=Tokyo|year=1988|edition=First|pages=111–112|chapter=14. Eight Peaks: Yatsu-ga-take 八ヶ岳|isbn=0-87011-893-5}}</ref>
The volcanoes consist mostly of pyroxene andesite. Yatsugatake also lies nearby to the Fossa Magna ([[:ja:フォッサマグナ|ja]]).<ref name="Hunt" />
==Local legend== According to legend, Yatsugatake was once higher than [[:en:Mount Fuji|Mount Fuji]], but [[Konohanasakuya-hime]], the goddess of Mount Fuji, tore it down out of jealousy, leaving the collection of peaks that exists today. Paul Hunt muses that this might not be far from the truth considering that Yatsugatake is older than Fuji and as Fuji rose in prominence Yatsugatake wore away.<ref name="Hunt" />
==Ecology== Deciduous forests reach up 800-1700m above sea level. Above this zone is the [[:en:subalpine zone|subalpine zone]] reaching up to 2500m. [[:en:Siberian dwarf pine|Siberian dwarf pine]] inhabits this subalpine zone. On one part of Mount Nishi under 1700m grows a rare form of spruce, ''[[:en:Picea koyamae|Picea koyamae]]''. [[:en:Rock Ptarmigan|Rock Ptarmigan]] (''Lagopus muta'') are also native to these mountains, but are under threat due to loss of the Siberian dwarf pine habitat and also due to expansion of the range of the [[:en:Red fox|Red fox]]. Humans have enabled the expansion of the red fox by leaving trash behind in the mountains. Also growing in the subalpine zone are ''[[:en:Abies veitchii|Abies veitchii]]'' and ''[[:en:Abies mariesii|Abies mariesii]]''.
==Gallery== <gallery mode=packed style="font-size:90%"> File:高根町から見た八ヶ岳 - panoramio.jpg|Southern Yatsugatake Volcanic Group from the SSE Minami-Yatsugatake from Mt.Kitayokodake 04.jpg|Southern Yatsugatake from the NNW File:Tengudake7.JPG|Northern Yatsugatake Volcanic Group from the S File:Mt.Akadake from Mt.Yokodake 06.jpg|[[:en:Mount Aka (Yatsugatake)|Mount Aka]] File:Mt.Amidadake from Mt.Yokodake 10.jpg|Mount Amida File:Tateshinayama-fuyu.JPG|[[:en:Mount Tateshina|Mount Tateshina]] File:Yatsugatake-Kogen-Higashizawa-Bridge.jpg|Mt. Yatsugatake and Higashizawa Bridge [[:en:Hokuto, Yamanashi|Hokuto]], [[:en:Yamanashi Prefecture|Yamanashi]] </gallery>
==References== {{reflist}}

{{coord|35.9708|N|138.3700|E|source:wikidata|display=title}}

| name= Yatsugatake Mountains | native_name={{native name|ja|八ヶ岳連峰}} | other_name= | etymology= Mountain with eight peaks<ref name="Hunt" />
| photo= Mount Yatsu.JPG | photo_caption= Yatsugatake Mountains, viewed from [[:en:Minamimaki, Nagano|Minamimaki, Nagano]].
| country= Japan | subdivision1_type= States | subdivision1= {{enum|Nagano Prefecture|Yamanashi Prefecture}} | subdivision2_type= Region | subdivision2= [[:en:Chūbu region|Chūbu]]
| highest= [[:en:Mount Aka (Yatsugatake)|Mount Aka]] | elevation_m= 2899 | coordinates=
| length_km= 30 | length_orientation= North-South | width_km= | width_orientation= | area_km2=
| geology= Volcanic | orogeny= Island arc | age= Quaternary | biome= Alpine climate
| map= | map_caption= }}
The {{nihongo|'''Yatsugatake Mountains'''|八ヶ岳連峰|Yatsugatake-renpō}} are a volcanic mountain range on the border between [[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]] and [[:en:Yamanashi Prefecture|Yamanashi Prefecture]] on the island of [[Honshu|Honshū]] in [[:en:Japan|Japan]].<ref name="Hunt" />
==Description== The mountain range consists of two volcanic groups, [[:en:Northern Yatsugatake Volcanic Group|Northern Yatsugatake Volcanic Group]] and [[:en:Southern Yatsugatake Volcanic Group|Southern Yatsugatake Volcanic Group]]. The two volcanic groups of Yatsugatake, North and South, have different characteristics. The North Yatsugatake mountains are gradual and lower while the South Yatsugatake mountains are steep and are more alpine in nature.
[[File:Yatsugatake Volcanic Chain 3D 2012 a.jpg|left|thumb|150px|Yatsugatake Mountains]] The southern group ranges from the [[Natsuzawa Pass]] to the end of the mountain range. The northern range includes [[:en:Mount Yoko (Northern Yatsugatake)|Mount Yoko]] to the other end of the range. [[:en:Mount Tateshina|Mount Tateshina]] is also included.
These mountains form a major part of the [[:en:Yatsugatake-Chūshin Kōgen Quasi-National Park|Yatsugatake-Chūshin Kōgen Quasi-National Park]].<ref name="MoEJ">{{cite web|url=http://www.biodic.go.jp/jpark/qnp/yatugata.html|script-title=ja:八ヶ岳中信高原国定公園|work=Japan Integrated Biodiversity Information System|publisher=Ministry of the Environment, Government of Japan|language=ja|access-date=2008-09-05}}</ref>
==Geology== These mountains consist of older eroded [[:en:Complex volcano|Complex volcano]]es, which were made by repeated volcanic eruptions over many thousands years. The last eruption was estimated from 600 to 800 years ago at Mount Yoko of North Yatsugatake Mountains. This is the only remaining active volcano.<ref name="QVoJ">{{cite web|url=http://riodb02.ibase.aist.go.jp/strata/VOL_JP/EN/vol/65.htm|title=KITA YATSU-GA-TAKE (TATESHINA-YAMA, YOKO-DAKE etc.)|year=2006|work=Quaternary Volcanoes of Japan|publisher=Geological Survey of Japan, AIST|access-date=2008-08-21}}</ref> The other volcanoes are extinct.<ref name="Hunt">{{cite book|last=Hunt|first=Paul|title=Hiking in Japan: An Adventurer's Guide to the Mountain Trails|publisher=Kodansha International Ltd.|location=Tokyo|year=1988|edition=First|pages=111–112|chapter=14. Eight Peaks: Yatsu-ga-take 八ヶ岳|isbn=0-87011-893-5}}</ref>
The volcanoes consist mostly of pyroxene andesite. Yatsugatake also lies nearby to the Fossa Magna ([[:ja:フォッサマグナ|ja]]).<ref name="Hunt" />
==Local legend== According to legend, Yatsugatake was once higher than [[:en:Mount Fuji|Mount Fuji]], but [[Konohanasakuya-hime]], the goddess of Mount Fuji, tore it down out of jealousy, leaving the collection of peaks that exists today. Paul Hunt muses that this might not be far from the truth considering that Yatsugatake is older than Fuji and as Fuji rose in prominence Yatsugatake wore away.<ref name="Hunt" />
==Ecology== Deciduous forests reach up 800-1700m above sea level. Above this zone is the [[:en:subalpine zone|subalpine zone]] reaching up to 2500m. [[:en:Siberian dwarf pine|Siberian dwarf pine]] inhabits this subalpine zone. On one part of Mount Nishi under 1700m grows a rare form of spruce, ''[[:en:Picea koyamae|Picea koyamae]]''. [[:en:Rock Ptarmigan|Rock Ptarmigan]] (''Lagopus muta'') are also native to these mountains, but are under threat due to loss of the Siberian dwarf pine habitat and also due to expansion of the range of the [[:en:Red fox|Red fox]]. Humans have enabled the expansion of the red fox by leaving trash behind in the mountains. Also growing in the subalpine zone are ''[[:en:Abies veitchii|Abies veitchii]]'' and ''[[:en:Abies mariesii|Abies mariesii]]''.
==Gallery== <gallery mode=packed style="font-size:90%"> File:高根町から見た八ヶ岳 - panoramio.jpg|Southern Yatsugatake Volcanic Group from the SSE Minami-Yatsugatake from Mt.Kitayokodake 04.jpg|Southern Yatsugatake from the NNW File:Tengudake7.JPG|Northern Yatsugatake Volcanic Group from the S File:Mt.Akadake from Mt.Yokodake 06.jpg|[[:en:Mount Aka (Yatsugatake)|Mount Aka]] File:Mt.Amidadake from Mt.Yokodake 10.jpg|Mount Amida File:Tateshinayama-fuyu.JPG|[[:en:Mount Tateshina|Mount Tateshina]] File:Yatsugatake-Kogen-Higashizawa-Bridge.jpg|Mt. Yatsugatake and Higashizawa Bridge [[:en:Hokuto, Yamanashi|Hokuto]], [[:en:Yamanashi Prefecture|Yamanashi]] </gallery>
==References== {{reflist}}

{{coord|35.9708|N|138.3700|E|source:wikidata|display=title}}
‎]]

| photo= Mount Yatsu.JPG | photo_caption= Yatsugatake Mountains, viewed from [[:en:Minamimaki, Nagano|Minamimaki, Nagano]].
| country= Japan | subdivision1_type= States | subdivision1= {{enum|Nagano Prefecture|Yamanashi Prefecture}} | subdivision2_type= Region | subdivision2= [[:en:Chūbu region|Chūbu]]
| highest= [[:en:Mount Aka (Yatsugatake)|Mount Aka]] | elevation_m= 2899 | coordinates=
| length_km= 30 | length_orientation= North-South | width_km= | width_orientation= | area_km2=
| geology= Volcanic | orogeny= Island arc | age= Quaternary | biome= Alpine climate
| map= | map_caption= }}
The {{nihongo|'''Yatsugatake Mountains'''|八ヶ岳連峰|Yatsugatake-renpō}} are a volcanic mountain range on the border between [[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]] and [[:en:Yamanashi Prefecture|Yamanashi Prefecture]] on the island of [[Honshu|Honshū]] in [[:en:Japan|Japan]].<ref name="Hunt" />
==Description== The mountain range consists of two volcanic groups, [[:en:Northern Yatsugatake Volcanic Group|Northern Yatsugatake Volcanic Group]] and [[:en:Southern Yatsugatake Volcanic Group|Southern Yatsugatake Volcanic Group]]. The two volcanic groups of Yatsugatake, North and South, have different characteristics. The North Yatsugatake mountains are gradual and lower while the South Yatsugatake mountains are steep and are more alpine in nature.
[[File:Yatsugatake Volcanic Chain 3D 2012 a.jpg|left|thumb|150px|Yatsugatake Mountains]] The southern group ranges from the [[Natsuzawa Pass]] to the end of the mountain range. The northern range includes [[:en:Mount Yoko (Northern Yatsugatake)|Mount Yoko]] to the other end of the range. [[:en:Mount Tateshina|Mount Tateshina]] is also included.
These mountains form a major part of the [[:en:Yatsugatake-Chūshin Kōgen Quasi-National Park|Yatsugatake-Chūshin Kōgen Quasi-National Park]].<ref name="MoEJ">{{cite web|url=http://www.biodic.go.jp/jpark/qnp/yatugata.html|script-title=ja:八ヶ岳中信高原国定公園|work=Japan Integrated Biodiversity Information System|publisher=Ministry of the Environment, Government of Japan|language=ja|access-date=2008-09-05}}</ref>
==Geology== These mountains consist of older eroded [[:en:Complex volcano|Complex volcano]]es, which were made by repeated volcanic eruptions over many thousands years. The last eruption was estimated from 600 to 800 years ago at Mount Yoko of North Yatsugatake Mountains. This is the only remaining active volcano.<ref name="QVoJ">{{cite web|url=http://riodb02.ibase.aist.go.jp/strata/VOL_JP/EN/vol/65.htm|title=KITA YATSU-GA-TAKE (TATESHINA-YAMA, YOKO-DAKE etc.)|year=2006|work=Quaternary Volcanoes of Japan|publisher=Geological Survey of Japan, AIST|access-date=2008-08-21}}</ref> The other volcanoes are extinct.<ref name="Hunt">{{cite book|last=Hunt|first=Paul|title=Hiking in Japan: An Adventurer's Guide to the Mountain Trails|publisher=Kodansha International Ltd.|location=Tokyo|year=1988|edition=First|pages=111–112|chapter=14. Eight Peaks: Yatsu-ga-take 八ヶ岳|isbn=0-87011-893-5}}</ref>
The volcanoes consist mostly of pyroxene andesite. Yatsugatake also lies nearby to the Fossa Magna ([[:ja:フォッサマグナ|ja]]).<ref name="Hunt" />
==Local legend== According to legend, Yatsugatake was once higher than [[:en:Mount Fuji|Mount Fuji]], but [[Konohanasakuya-hime]], the goddess of Mount Fuji, tore it down out of jealousy, leaving the collection of peaks that exists today. Paul Hunt muses that this might not be far from the truth considering that Yatsugatake is older than Fuji and as Fuji rose in prominence Yatsugatake wore away.<ref name="Hunt" />
==Ecology== Deciduous forests reach up 800-1700m above sea level. Above this zone is the [[:en:subalpine zone|subalpine zone]] reaching up to 2500m. [[:en:Siberian dwarf pine|Siberian dwarf pine]] inhabits this subalpine zone. On one part of Mount Nishi under 1700m grows a rare form of spruce, ''[[:en:Picea koyamae|Picea koyamae]]''. [[:en:Rock Ptarmigan|Rock Ptarmigan]] (''Lagopus muta'') are also native to these mountains, but are under threat due to loss of the Siberian dwarf pine habitat and also due to expansion of the range of the [[:en:Red fox|Red fox]]. Humans have enabled the expansion of the red fox by leaving trash behind in the mountains. Also growing in the subalpine zone are ''[[:en:Abies veitchii|Abies veitchii]]'' and ''[[:en:Abies mariesii|Abies mariesii]]''.
==Gallery== <gallery mode=packed style="font-size:90%"> File:高根町から見た八ヶ岳 - panoramio.jpg|Southern Yatsugatake Volcanic Group from the SSE Minami-Yatsugatake from Mt.Kitayokodake 04.jpg|Southern Yatsugatake from the NNW File:Tengudake7.JPG|Northern Yatsugatake Volcanic Group from the S File:Mt.Akadake from Mt.Yokodake 06.jpg|[[:en:Mount Aka (Yatsugatake)|Mount Aka]] File:Mt.Amidadake from Mt.Yokodake 10.jpg|Mount Amida File:Tateshinayama-fuyu.JPG|[[:en:Mount Tateshina|Mount Tateshina]] File:Yatsugatake-Kogen-Higashizawa-Bridge.jpg|Mt. Yatsugatake and Higashizawa Bridge [[:en:Hokuto, Yamanashi|Hokuto]], [[:en:Yamanashi Prefecture|Yamanashi]] </gallery>

Yatsugatake and Higashizawa Bridge [[:en:Hokuto, Yamanashi|Hokuto]], [[:en:Yamanashi Prefecture|Yamanashi]] </gallery>

revision.comment: v25: Convert interwiki links [[:en:...]] to {{ill}} templates
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[arz:جبال ياتسوجاتاكى]][[en:Yatsugatake Mountains]][[fa:کوه یاتسوداتاکه]][[fr:Monts Yatsugatake]][[ja:八ヶ岳連峰]][[lld:Ciadëina de Yatsugatake]][[sco:Yatsugatake Bens]][[zh:八岳連峰]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[arz:جبال ياتسوجاتاكى]][[en:Yatsugatake Mountains]][[fa:کوه یاتسوداتاکه]][[fr:Monts Yatsugatake]][[ja:八ヶ岳連峰]][[lld:Ciadëina de Yatsugatake]][[sco:Yatsugatake Bens]][[zh:八岳連峰]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[arz:جبال ياتسوجاتاكى]][[en:Yatsugatake Mountains]][[fa:کوه یاتسوداتاکه]][[fr:Monts Yatsugatake]][[ja:八ヶ岳連峰]][[lld:Ciadëina de Yatsugatake]][[sco:Yatsugatake Bens]][[zh:八岳連峰]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11390433]] Wikidata name: Yatsugatake Mountains [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:30, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Volcanic mountain range in Japan}} {{Infobox mountain
| name= Yatsugatake Mountains | native_name={{native name|ja|八ヶ岳連峰}} | other_name= | etymology= Mountain with eight peaks<ref name="Hunt" />
| photo= Mount Yatsu.JPG | photo_caption= Yatsugatake Mountains, viewed from {{ill|Minamimaki, Nagano|en|Minamimaki, Nagano|WD=Q1203240|lt=Minamimaki, Nagano}}.
| country= Japan | subdivision1_type= States | subdivision1= {{enum|Nagano Prefecture|Yamanashi Prefecture}} | subdivision2_type= Region | subdivision2= {{ill|Chūbu region|en|Chūbu region|WD=Q134638|lt=Chūbu}}
| highest= {{ill|Mount Aka (Yatsugatake)|en|Mount Aka (Yatsugatake)|WD=Q3915982|lt=Mount Aka}} | elevation_m= 2899 | coordinates=
| length_km= 30 | length_orientation= North-South | width_km= | width_orientation= | area_km2=
| geology= Volcanic | orogeny= Island arc | age= Quaternary | biome= Alpine climate
| map= | map_caption= }}
The {{nihongo|'''Yatsugatake Mountains'''|八ヶ岳連峰|Yatsugatake-renpō}} are a volcanic mountain range on the border between {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|en|Nagano Prefecture|WD=Q127877|lt=Nagano Prefecture}} and {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|en|Yamanashi Prefecture|WD=Q132720|lt=Yamanashi Prefecture}} on the island of [[Honshu|Honshū]] in {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}.<ref name="Hunt" />
==Description== The mountain range consists of two volcanic groups, {{ill|Northern Yatsugatake Volcanic Group|en|Northern Yatsugatake Volcanic Group|WD=Q7059149|lt=Northern Yatsugatake Volcanic Group}} and {{ill|Southern Yatsugatake Volcanic Group|en|Southern Yatsugatake Volcanic Group|WD=Q905588|lt=Southern Yatsugatake Volcanic Group}}. The two volcanic groups of Yatsugatake, North and South, have different characteristics. The North Yatsugatake mountains are gradual and lower while the South Yatsugatake mountains are steep and are more alpine in nature.
[[File:Yatsugatake Volcanic Chain 3D 2012 a.jpg|left|thumb|150px|Yatsugatake Mountains]] The southern group ranges from the [[Natsuzawa Pass]] to the end of the mountain range. The northern range includes {{ill|Mount Yoko (Northern Yatsugatake)|en|Mount Yoko (Northern Yatsugatake)|WD=Q6924704|lt=Mount Yoko}} to the other end of the range. {{ill|Mount Tateshina|en|Mount Tateshina|WD=Q717453|lt=Mount Tateshina}} is also included.
These mountains form a major part of the {{ill|Yatsugatake-Chūshin Kōgen Quasi-National Park|en|Yatsugatake-Chūshin Kōgen Quasi-National Park|WD=Q1104617|lt=Yatsugatake-Chūshin Kōgen Quasi-National Park}}.<ref name="MoEJ">{{cite web|url=http://www.biodic.go.jp/jpark/qnp/yatugata.html|script-title=ja:八ヶ岳中信高原国定公園|work=Japan Integrated Biodiversity Information System|publisher=Ministry of the Environment, Government of Japan|language=ja|access-date=2008-09-05}}</ref>
==Geology== These mountains consist of older eroded {{ill|Complex volcano|en|Complex volcano|WD=Q1200524|lt=Complex volcano}}es, which were made by repeated volcanic eruptions over many thousands years. The last eruption was estimated from 600 to 800 years ago at Mount Yoko of North Yatsugatake Mountains. This is the only remaining active volcano.<ref name="QVoJ">{{cite web|url=http://riodb02.ibase.aist.go.jp/strata/VOL_JP/EN/vol/65.htm|title=KITA YATSU-GA-TAKE (TATESHINA-YAMA, YOKO-DAKE etc.)|year=2006|work=Quaternary Volcanoes of Japan|publisher=Geological Survey of Japan, AIST|access-date=2008-08-21}}</ref> The other volcanoes are extinct.<ref name="Hunt">{{cite book|last=Hunt|first=Paul|title=Hiking in Japan: An Adventurer's Guide to the Mountain Trails|publisher=Kodansha International Ltd.|location=Tokyo|year=1988|edition=First|pages=111–112|chapter=14. Eight Peaks: Yatsu-ga-take 八ヶ岳|isbn=0-87011-893-5}}</ref>
The volcanoes consist mostly of pyroxene andesite. Yatsugatake also lies nearby to the Fossa Magna ([[:ja:フォッサマグナ|ja]]).<ref name="Hunt" />
==Local legend== According to legend, Yatsugatake was once higher than {{ill|Mount Fuji|en|Mount Fuji|WD=Q39231|lt=Mount Fuji}}, but [[Konohanasakuya-hime]], the goddess of Mount Fuji, tore it down out of jealousy, leaving the collection of peaks that exists today. Paul Hunt muses that this might not be far from the truth considering that Yatsugatake is older than Fuji and as Fuji rose in prominence Yatsugatake wore away.<ref name="Hunt" />
==Ecology== Deciduous forests reach up 800-1700m above sea level. Above this zone is the [[:en:subalpine zone|subalpine zone]] reaching up to 2500m. [[:en:Siberian dwarf pine|Siberian dwarf pine]] inhabits this subalpine zone. On one part of Mount Nishi under 1700m grows a rare form of spruce, ''{{ill|Picea koyamae|en|Picea koyamae|WD=Q391374|lt=Picea koyamae}}''. [[:en:Rock Ptarmigan|Rock Ptarmigan]] (''Lagopus muta'') are also native to these mountains, but are under threat due to loss of the Siberian dwarf pine habitat and also due to expansion of the range of the {{ill|Red fox|en|Red fox|WD=Q8332|lt=Red fox}}. Humans have enabled the expansion of the red fox by leaving trash behind in the mountains. Also growing in the subalpine zone are ''{{ill|Abies veitchii|en|Abies veitchii|WD=Q1542823|lt=Abies veitchii}}'' and ''{{ill|Abies mariesii|en|Abies mariesii|WD=Q1614934|lt=Abies mariesii}}''.
==Gallery== <gallery mode=packed style="font-size:90%"> File:高根町から見た八ヶ岳 - panoramio.jpg|Southern Yatsugatake Volcanic Group from the SSE Minami-Yatsugatake from Mt.Kitayokodake 04.jpg|Southern Yatsugatake from the NNW File:Tengudake7.JPG|Northern Yatsugatake Volcanic Group from the S File:Mt.Akadake from Mt.Yokodake 06.jpg|{{ill|Mount Aka (Yatsugatake)|en|Mount Aka (Yatsugatake)|WD=Q3915982|lt=Mount Aka}} File:Mt.Amidadake from Mt.Yokodake 10.jpg|Mount Amida File:Tateshinayama-fuyu.JPG|{{ill|Mount Tateshina|en|Mount Tateshina|WD=Q717453|lt=Mount Tateshina}} File:Yatsugatake-Kogen-Higashizawa-Bridge.jpg|Mt. Yatsugatake and Higashizawa Bridge {{ill|Hokuto, Yamanashi|en|Hokuto, Yamanashi|WD=Q825272|lt=Hokuto}}, {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|en|Yamanashi Prefecture|WD=Q132720|lt=Yamanashi}} </gallery>
==References== {{reflist}}

{{coord|35.9708|N|138.3700|E|source:wikidata|display=title}}
‎]]

Eight Peaks: Yatsu-ga-take 八ヶ岳|isbn=0-87011-893-5}}</ref>

The North Yatsugatake mountains are gradual and lower while the South Yatsugatake mountains are steep and are more alpine in nature.
[[File:Yatsugatake Volcanic Chain 3D 2012 a.jpg|left|thumb|150px|Yatsugatake Mountains]] The southern group ranges from the [[Natsuzawa Pass]] to the end of the mountain range. The northern range includes {{ill|Mount Yoko (Northern Yatsugatake)|en|Mount Yoko (Northern Yatsugatake)|WD=Q6924704|lt=Mount Yoko}} to the other end of the range. {{ill|Mount Tateshina|en|Mount Tateshina|WD=Q717453|lt=Mount Tateshina}} is also included.
These mountains form a major part of the {{ill|Yatsugatake-Chūshin Kōgen Quasi-National Park|en|Yatsugatake-Chūshin Kōgen Quasi-National Park|WD=Q1104617|lt=Yatsugatake-Chūshin Kōgen Quasi-National Park}}.<ref name="MoEJ">{{cite web|url=http://www.biodic.go.jp/jpark/qnp/yatugata.html|script-title=ja:八ヶ岳中信高原国定公園|work=Japan Integrated Biodiversity Information System|publisher=Ministry of the Environment, Government of Japan|language=ja|access-date=2008-09-05}}</ref>
==Geology== These mountains consist of older eroded {{ill|Complex volcano|en|Complex volcano|WD=Q1200524|lt=Complex volcano}}es, which were made by repeated volcanic eruptions over many thousands years. The last eruption was estimated from 600 to 800 years ago at Mount Yoko of North Yatsugatake Mountains. This is the only remaining active volcano.<ref name="QVoJ">{{cite web|url=http://riodb02.ibase.aist.go.jp/strata/VOL_JP/EN/vol/65.htm|title=KITA YATSU-GA-TAKE (TATESHINA-YAMA, YOKO-DAKE etc.)|year=2006|work=Quaternary Volcanoes of Japan|publisher=Geological Survey of Japan, AIST|access-date=2008-08-21}}</ref> The other volcanoes are extinct.<ref name="Hunt">{{cite book|last=Hunt|first=Paul|title=Hiking in Japan: An Adventurer's Guide to the Mountain Trails|publisher=Kodansha International Ltd.|location=Tokyo|year=1988|edition=First|pages=111–112|chapter=14. Eight Peaks: Yatsu-ga-take 八ヶ岳|isbn=0-87011-893-5}}</ref>
The volcanoes consist mostly of pyroxene andesite. Yatsugatake also lies nearby to the Fossa Magna ([[:ja:フォッサマグナ|ja]]).<ref name="Hunt" />
==Local legend== According to legend, Yatsugatake was once higher than {{ill|Mount Fuji|en|Mount Fuji|WD=Q39231|lt=Mount Fuji}}, but [[Konohanasakuya-hime]], the goddess of Mount Fuji, tore it down out of jealousy, leaving the collection of peaks that exists today. Paul Hunt muses that this might not be far from the truth considering that Yatsugatake is older than Fuji and as Fuji rose in prominence Yatsugatake wore away.<ref name="Hunt" />
==Ecology== Deciduous forests reach up 800-1700m above sea level. Above this zone is the [[:en:subalpine zone|subalpine zone]] reaching up to 2500m. [[:en:Siberian dwarf pine|Siberian dwarf pine]] inhabits this subalpine zone. On one part of Mount Nishi under 1700m grows a rare form of spruce, ''{{ill|Picea koyamae|en|Picea koyamae|WD=Q391374|lt=Picea koyamae}}''. [[:en:Rock Ptarmigan|Rock Ptarmigan]] (''Lagopus muta'') are also native to these mountains, but are under threat due to loss of the Siberian dwarf pine habitat and also due to expansion of the range of the {{ill|Red fox|en|Red fox|WD=Q8332|lt=Red fox}}. Humans have enabled the expansion of the red fox by leaving trash behind in the mountains. Also growing in the subalpine zone are ''{{ill|Abies veitchii|en|Abies veitchii|WD=Q1542823|lt=Abies veitchii}}'' and ''{{ill|Abies mariesii|en|Abies mariesii|WD=Q1614934|lt=Abies mariesii}}''.
==Gallery== <gallery mode=packed style="font-size:90%"> File:高根町から見た八ヶ岳 - panoramio.jpg|Southern Yatsugatake Volcanic Group from the SSE Minami-Yatsugatake from Mt.Kitayokodake 04.jpg|Southern Yatsugatake from the NNW File:Tengudake7.JPG|Northern Yatsugatake Volcanic Group from the S File:Mt.Akadake from Mt.Yokodake 06.jpg|{{ill|Mount Aka (Yatsugatake)|en|Mount Aka (Yatsugatake)|WD=Q3915982|lt=Mount Aka}} File:Mt.Amidadake from Mt.Yokodake 10.jpg|Mount Amida File:Tateshinayama-fuyu.JPG|{{ill|Mount Tateshina|en|Mount Tateshina|WD=Q717453|lt=Mount Tateshina}} File:Yatsugatake-Kogen-Higashizawa-Bridge.jpg|Mt. Yatsugatake and Higashizawa Bridge {{ill|Hokuto, Yamanashi|en|Hokuto, Yamanashi|WD=Q825272|lt=Hokuto}}, {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|en|Yamanashi Prefecture|WD=Q132720|lt=Yamanashi}} </gallery>

The {{nihongo|'''Yatsugatake Mountains'''|八ヶ岳連峰|Yatsugatake-renpō}} are a volcanic mountain range on the border between {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|en|Nagano Prefecture|WD=Q127877|lt=Nagano Prefecture}} and {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|en|Yamanashi Prefecture|WD=Q132720|lt=Yamanashi Prefecture}} on the island of [[Honshu|Honshū]] in {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}.<ref name="Hunt" />
==Description== The mountain range consists of two volcanic groups, {{ill|Northern Yatsugatake Volcanic Group|en|Northern Yatsugatake Volcanic Group|WD=Q7059149|lt=Northern Yatsugatake Volcanic Group}} and {{ill|Southern Yatsugatake Volcanic Group|en|Southern Yatsugatake Volcanic Group|WD=Q905588|lt=Southern Yatsugatake Volcanic Group}}. The two volcanic groups of Yatsugatake, North and South, have different characteristics. The North Yatsugatake mountains are gradual and lower while the South Yatsugatake mountains are steep and are more alpine in nature.
[[File:Yatsugatake Volcanic Chain 3D 2012 a.jpg|left|thumb|150px|Yatsugatake Mountains]] The southern group ranges from the [[Natsuzawa Pass]] to the end of the mountain range. The northern range includes {{ill|Mount Yoko (Northern Yatsugatake)|en|Mount Yoko (Northern Yatsugatake)|WD=Q6924704|lt=Mount Yoko}} to the other end of the range. {{ill|Mount Tateshina|en|Mount Tateshina|WD=Q717453|lt=Mount Tateshina}} is also included.
These mountains form a major part of the {{ill|Yatsugatake-Chūshin Kōgen Quasi-National Park|en|Yatsugatake-Chūshin Kōgen Quasi-National Park|WD=Q1104617|lt=Yatsugatake-Chūshin Kōgen Quasi-National Park}}.<ref name="MoEJ">{{cite web|url=http://www.biodic.go.jp/jpark/qnp/yatugata.html|script-title=ja:八ヶ岳中信高原国定公園|work=Japan Integrated Biodiversity Information System|publisher=Ministry of the Environment, Government of Japan|language=ja|access-date=2008-09-05}}</ref>

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[arz:جبال ياتسوجاتاكى]][[en:Yatsugatake Mountains]][[fa:کوه یاتسوداتاکه]][[fr:Monts Yatsugatake]][[ja:八ヶ岳連峰]][[lld:Ciadëina de Yatsugatake]][[sco:Yatsugatake Bens]][[zh:八岳連峰]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[arz:جبال ياتسوجاتاكى]][[en:Yatsugatake Mountains]][[fa:کوه یاتسوداتاکه]][[fr:Monts Yatsugatake]][[ja:八ヶ岳連峰]][[lld:Ciadëina de Yatsugatake]][[sco:Yatsugatake Bens]][[zh:八岳連峰]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[arz:جبال ياتسوجاتاكى]][[en:Yatsugatake Mountains]][[fa:کوه یاتسوداتاکه]][[fr:Monts Yatsugatake]][[ja:八ヶ岳連峰]][[lld:Ciadëina de Yatsugatake]][[sco:Yatsugatake Bens]][[zh:八岳連峰]]
{{Short description|Volcanic mountain range in Japan}} {{Infobox mountain
| name= Yatsugatake Mountains | native_name={{native name|ja|八ヶ岳連峰}} | other_name= | etymology= Mountain with eight peaks<ref name="Hunt" />
| photo= Mount Yatsu.JPG | photo_caption= Yatsugatake Mountains, viewed from {{ill|Minamimaki, Nagano|en|Minamimaki, Nagano|WD=Q1203240|lt=Minamimaki, Nagano}}.
| country= Japan | subdivision1_type= States | subdivision1= {{enum|Nagano Prefecture|Yamanashi Prefecture}} | subdivision2_type= Region | subdivision2= {{ill|Chūbu region|en|Chūbu region|WD=Q134638|lt=Chūbu}}
| highest= {{ill|Mount Aka (Yatsugatake)|en|Mount Aka (Yatsugatake)|WD=Q3915982|lt=Mount Aka}} | elevation_m= 2899 | coordinates=
| length_km= 30 | length_orientation= North-South | width_km= | width_orientation= | area_km2=
| geology= Volcanic | orogeny= Island arc | age= Quaternary | biome= Alpine climate
| map= | map_caption= }}
The {{nihongo|'''Yatsugatake Mountains'''|八ヶ岳連峰|Yatsugatake-renpō}} are a volcanic mountain range on the border between {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|en|Nagano Prefecture|WD=Q127877|lt=Nagano Prefecture}} and {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|en|Yamanashi Prefecture|WD=Q132720|lt=Yamanashi Prefecture}} on the island of [[Honshu|Honshū]] in {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}.<ref name="Hunt" />
==Description== The mountain range consists of two volcanic groups, {{ill|Northern Yatsugatake Volcanic Group|en|Northern Yatsugatake Volcanic Group|WD=Q7059149|lt=Northern Yatsugatake Volcanic Group}} and {{ill|Southern Yatsugatake Volcanic Group|en|Southern Yatsugatake Volcanic Group|WD=Q905588|lt=Southern Yatsugatake Volcanic Group}}. The two volcanic groups of Yatsugatake, North and South, have different characteristics. The North Yatsugatake mountains are gradual and lower while the South Yatsugatake mountains are steep and are more alpine in nature.
[[File:Yatsugatake Volcanic Chain 3D 2012 a.jpg|left|thumb|150px|Yatsugatake Mountains]] The southern group ranges from the [[Natsuzawa Pass]] to the end of the mountain range. The northern range includes {{ill|Mount Yoko (Northern Yatsugatake)|en|Mount Yoko (Northern Yatsugatake)|WD=Q6924704|lt=Mount Yoko}} to the other end of the range. {{ill|Mount Tateshina|en|Mount Tateshina|WD=Q717453|lt=Mount Tateshina}} is also included.
These mountains form a major part of the {{ill|Yatsugatake-Chūshin Kōgen Quasi-National Park|en|Yatsugatake-Chūshin Kōgen Quasi-National Park|WD=Q1104617|lt=Yatsugatake-Chūshin Kōgen Quasi-National Park}}.<ref name="MoEJ">{{cite web|url=http://www.biodic.go.jp/jpark/qnp/yatugata.html|script-title=ja:八ヶ岳中信高原国定公園|work=Japan Integrated Biodiversity Information System|publisher=Ministry of the Environment, Government of Japan|language=ja|access-date=2008-09-05}}</ref>
==Geology== These mountains consist of older eroded {{ill|Complex volcano|en|Complex volcano|WD=Q1200524|lt=Complex volcano}}es, which were made by repeated volcanic eruptions over many thousands years. The last eruption was estimated from 600 to 800 years ago at Mount Yoko of North Yatsugatake Mountains. This is the only remaining active volcano.<ref name="QVoJ">{{cite web|url=http://riodb02.ibase.aist.go.jp/strata/VOL_JP/EN/vol/65.htm|title=KITA YATSU-GA-TAKE (TATESHINA-YAMA, YOKO-DAKE etc.)|year=2006|work=Quaternary Volcanoes of Japan|publisher=Geological Survey of Japan, AIST|access-date=2008-08-21}}</ref> The other volcanoes are extinct.<ref name="Hunt">{{cite book|last=Hunt|first=Paul|title=Hiking in Japan: An Adventurer's Guide to the Mountain Trails|publisher=Kodansha International Ltd.|location=Tokyo|year=1988|edition=First|pages=111–112|chapter=14. Eight Peaks: Yatsu-ga-take 八ヶ岳|isbn=0-87011-893-5}}</ref>
The volcanoes consist mostly of pyroxene andesite. Yatsugatake also lies nearby to the Fossa Magna ([[:ja:フォッサマグナ|ja]]).<ref name="Hunt" />
==Local legend== According to legend, Yatsugatake was once higher than {{ill|Mount Fuji|en|Mount Fuji|WD=Q39231|lt=Mount Fuji}}, but [[Konohanasakuya-hime]], the goddess of Mount Fuji, tore it down out of jealousy, leaving the collection of peaks that exists today. Paul Hunt muses that this might not be far from the truth considering that Yatsugatake is older than Fuji and as Fuji rose in prominence Yatsugatake wore away.<ref name="Hunt" />
==Ecology== Deciduous forests reach up 800-1700m above sea level. Above this zone is the [[:en:subalpine zone|subalpine zone]] reaching up to 2500m. [[:en:Siberian dwarf pine|Siberian dwarf pine]] inhabits this subalpine zone. On one part of Mount Nishi under 1700m grows a rare form of spruce, ''{{ill|Picea koyamae|en|Picea koyamae|WD=Q391374|lt=Picea koyamae}}''. [[:en:Rock Ptarmigan|Rock Ptarmigan]] (''Lagopus muta'') are also native to these mountains, but are under threat due to loss of the Siberian dwarf pine habitat and also due to expansion of the range of the {{ill|Red fox|en|Red fox|WD=Q8332|lt=Red fox}}. Humans have enabled the expansion of the red fox by leaving trash behind in the mountains. Also growing in the subalpine zone are ''{{ill|Abies veitchii|en|Abies veitchii|WD=Q1542823|lt=Abies veitchii}}'' and ''{{ill|Abies mariesii|en|Abies mariesii|WD=Q1614934|lt=Abies mariesii}}''.
==Gallery== <gallery mode=packed style="font-size:90%"> File:高根町から見た八ヶ岳 - panoramio.jpg|Southern Yatsugatake Volcanic Group from the SSE Minami-Yatsugatake from Mt.Kitayokodake 04.jpg|Southern Yatsugatake from the NNW File:Tengudake7.JPG|Northern Yatsugatake Volcanic Group from the S File:Mt.Akadake from Mt.Yokodake 06.jpg|{{ill|Mount Aka (Yatsugatake)|en|Mount Aka (Yatsugatake)|WD=Q3915982|lt=Mount Aka}} File:Mt.Amidadake from Mt.Yokodake 10.jpg|Mount Amida File:Tateshinayama-fuyu.JPG|{{ill|Mount Tateshina|en|Mount Tateshina|WD=Q717453|lt=Mount Tateshina}} File:Yatsugatake-Kogen-Higashizawa-Bridge.jpg|Mt. Yatsugatake and Higashizawa Bridge {{ill|Hokuto, Yamanashi|en|Hokuto, Yamanashi|WD=Q825272|lt=Hokuto}}, {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|en|Yamanashi Prefecture|WD=Q132720|lt=Yamanashi}} </gallery>
==References== {{reflist}}
== External links == {{commons Category|Yatsugatake Mountains}} * [https://gbank.gsj.jp/volcano/Quat_Vol/volcano_data/E84.html Kita Yatsugatake (Northern Yatsugatake)] - Geological Survey of Japan * [https://gbank.gsj.jp/volcano/Quat_Vol/gallery/E85.htm Minami Yatsugatake (Southern Yatsugatake)] - Geological Survey of Japan * {{cite gvp|name=Yatsugatake|vn=283824|access-date=2021-06-24}}
{{coord|35.9708|N|138.3700|E|source:wikidata|display=title}}
‎]]

[[:en:Rock Ptarmigan|Rock Ptarmigan]] (''Lagopus muta'') are also native to these mountains, but are under threat due to loss of the Siberian dwarf pine habitat and also due to expansion of the range of the {{ill|Red fox|en|Red fox|WD=Q8332|lt=Red fox|1=Red fox}}. Humans have enabled the expansion of the red fox by leaving trash behind in the mountains. Also growing in the subalpine zone are ''{{ill|Abies veitchii|en|Abies veitchii|WD=Q1542823|lt=Abies veitchii|1=Abies veitchii}}'' and ''{{ill|Abies mariesii|en|Abies mariesii|WD=Q1614934|lt=Abies mariesii|1=Abies mariesii}}''.

Yatsugatake and Higashizawa Bridge {{ill|Hokuto, Yamanashi|en|Hokuto, Yamanashi|WD=Q825272|lt=Hokuto|1=Hokuto, Yamanashi}}, {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|en|Yamanashi Prefecture|WD=Q132720|lt=Yamanashi|1=Yamanashi Prefecture}} </gallery>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[arz:جبال ياتسوجاتاكى]][[en:Yatsugatake Mountains]][[fa:کوه یاتسوداتاکه]][[fr:Monts Yatsugatake]][[ja:八ヶ岳連峰]][[lld:Ciadëina de Yatsugatake]][[sco:Yatsugatake Bens]][[zh:八岳連峰]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[arz:جبال ياتسوجاتاكى]][[en:Yatsugatake Mountains]][[fa:کوه یاتسوداتاکه]][[fr:Monts Yatsugatake]][[ja:八ヶ岳連峰]][[lld:Ciadëina de Yatsugatake]][[sco:Yatsugatake Bens]][[zh:八岳連峰]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[arz:جبال ياتسوجاتاكى]][[en:Yatsugatake Mountains]][[fa:کوه یاتسوداتاکه]][[fr:Monts Yatsugatake]][[ja:八ヶ岳連峰]][[lld:Ciadëina de Yatsugatake]][[sco:Yatsugatake Bens]][[zh:八岳連峰]]
{{Short description|Volcanic mountain range in Japan}} {{Infobox mountain
| name= Yatsugatake Mountains | native_name={{native name|ja|八ヶ岳連峰}} | other_name= | etymology= Mountain with eight peaks<ref name="Hunt" />
| photo= Mount Yatsu.JPG | photo_caption= Yatsugatake Mountains, viewed from {{ill|Minamimaki, Nagano|en|Minamimaki, Nagano|WD=Q1203240|lt=Minamimaki, Nagano|1=Minamimaki, Nagano}}.
| country= Japan | subdivision1_type= States | subdivision1= {{enum|Nagano Prefecture|Yamanashi Prefecture}} | subdivision2_type= Region | subdivision2= {{ill|Chūbu region|en|Chūbu region|WD=Q134638|lt=Chūbu|1=Chūbu region}}
| highest= {{ill|Mount Aka (Yatsugatake)|en|Mount Aka (Yatsugatake)|WD=Q3915982|lt=Mount Aka|1=Mount Aka (Yatsugatake)}} | elevation_m= 2899 | coordinates=
| length_km= 30 | length_orientation= North-South | width_km= | width_orientation= | area_km2=
| geology= Volcanic | orogeny= Island arc | age= Quaternary | biome= Alpine climate
| map= | map_caption= }}
The {{nihongo|'''Yatsugatake Mountains'''|八ヶ岳連峰|Yatsugatake-renpō}} are a volcanic mountain range on the border between {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|en|Nagano Prefecture|WD=Q127877|lt=Nagano Prefecture|1=Nagano Prefecture}} and {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|en|Yamanashi Prefecture|WD=Q132720|lt=Yamanashi Prefecture|1=Yamanashi Prefecture}} on the island of [[Honshu|Honshū]] in {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}.<ref name="Hunt" />
==Description== The mountain range consists of two volcanic groups, {{ill|Northern Yatsugatake Volcanic Group|en|Northern Yatsugatake Volcanic Group|WD=Q7059149|lt=Northern Yatsugatake Volcanic Group|1=Northern Yatsugatake Volcanic Group}} and {{ill|Southern Yatsugatake Volcanic Group|en|Southern Yatsugatake Volcanic Group|WD=Q905588|lt=Southern Yatsugatake Volcanic Group|1=Southern Yatsugatake Volcanic Group}}. The two volcanic groups of Yatsugatake, North and South, have different characteristics. The North Yatsugatake mountains are gradual and lower while the South Yatsugatake mountains are steep and are more alpine in nature.
[[File:Yatsugatake Volcanic Chain 3D 2012 a.jpg|left|thumb|150px|Yatsugatake Mountains]] The southern group ranges from the [[Natsuzawa Pass]] to the end of the mountain range. The northern range includes {{ill|Mount Yoko (Northern Yatsugatake)|en|Mount Yoko (Northern Yatsugatake)|WD=Q6924704|lt=Mount Yoko|1=Mount Yoko (Northern Yatsugatake)}} to the other end of the range. {{ill|Mount Tateshina|en|Mount Tateshina|WD=Q717453|lt=Mount Tateshina|1=Mount Tateshina}} is also included.
These mountains form a major part of the {{ill|Yatsugatake-Chūshin Kōgen Quasi-National Park|en|Yatsugatake-Chūshin Kōgen Quasi-National Park|WD=Q1104617|lt=Yatsugatake-Chūshin Kōgen Quasi-National Park|1=Yatsugatake-Chūshin Kōgen Quasi-National Park}}.<ref name="MoEJ">{{cite web|url=http://www.biodic.go.jp/jpark/qnp/yatugata.html|script-title=ja:八ヶ岳中信高原国定公園|work=Japan Integrated Biodiversity Information System|publisher=Ministry of the Environment, Government of Japan|language=ja|access-date=2008-09-05}}</ref>
==Geology== These mountains consist of older eroded {{ill|Complex volcano|en|Complex volcano|WD=Q1200524|lt=Complex volcano|1=Complex volcano}}es, which were made by repeated volcanic eruptions over many thousands years. The last eruption was estimated from 600 to 800 years ago at Mount Yoko of North Yatsugatake Mountains. This is the only remaining active volcano.<ref name="QVoJ">{{cite web|url=http://riodb02.ibase.aist.go.jp/strata/VOL_JP/EN/vol/65.htm|title=KITA YATSU-GA-TAKE (TATESHINA-YAMA, YOKO-DAKE etc.)|year=2006|work=Quaternary Volcanoes of Japan|publisher=Geological Survey of Japan, AIST|access-date=2008-08-21}}</ref> The other volcanoes are extinct.<ref name="Hunt">{{cite book|last=Hunt|first=Paul|title=Hiking in Japan: An Adventurer's Guide to the Mountain Trails|publisher=Kodansha International Ltd.|location=Tokyo|year=1988|edition=First|pages=111–112|chapter=14. Eight Peaks: Yatsu-ga-take 八ヶ岳|isbn=0-87011-893-5}}</ref>
The volcanoes consist mostly of pyroxene andesite. Yatsugatake also lies nearby to the Fossa Magna ([[:ja:フォッサマグナ|ja]]).<ref name="Hunt" />
==Local legend== According to legend, Yatsugatake was once higher than {{ill|Mount Fuji|en|Mount Fuji|WD=Q39231|lt=Mount Fuji|1=Mount Fuji}}, but [[Konohanasakuya-hime]], the goddess of Mount Fuji, tore it down out of jealousy, leaving the collection of peaks that exists today. Paul Hunt muses that this might not be far from the truth considering that Yatsugatake is older than Fuji and as Fuji rose in prominence Yatsugatake wore away.<ref name="Hunt" />
==Ecology== Deciduous forests reach up 800-1700m above sea level. Above this zone is the [[:en:subalpine zone|subalpine zone]] reaching up to 2500m. [[:en:Siberian dwarf pine|Siberian dwarf pine]] inhabits this subalpine zone. On one part of Mount Nishi under 1700m grows a rare form of spruce, ''{{ill|Picea koyamae|en|Picea koyamae|WD=Q391374|lt=Picea koyamae|1=Picea koyamae}}''. [[:en:Rock Ptarmigan|Rock Ptarmigan]] (''Lagopus muta'') are also native to these mountains, but are under threat due to loss of the Siberian dwarf pine habitat and also due to expansion of the range of the {{ill|Red fox|en|Red fox|WD=Q8332|lt=Red fox|1=Red fox}}. Humans have enabled the expansion of the red fox by leaving trash behind in the mountains. Also growing in the subalpine zone are ''{{ill|Abies veitchii|en|Abies veitchii|WD=Q1542823|lt=Abies veitchii|1=Abies veitchii}}'' and ''{{ill|Abies mariesii|en|Abies mariesii|WD=Q1614934|lt=Abies mariesii|1=Abies mariesii}}''.
==Gallery== <gallery mode=packed style="font-size:90%"> File:高根町から見た八ヶ岳 - panoramio.jpg|Southern Yatsugatake Volcanic Group from the SSE Minami-Yatsugatake from Mt.Kitayokodake 04.jpg|Southern Yatsugatake from the NNW File:Tengudake7.JPG|Northern Yatsugatake Volcanic Group from the S File:Mt.Akadake from Mt.Yokodake 06.jpg|{{ill|Mount Aka (Yatsugatake)|en|Mount Aka (Yatsugatake)|WD=Q3915982|lt=Mount Aka|1=Mount Aka (Yatsugatake)}} File:Mt.Amidadake from Mt.Yokodake 10.jpg|Mount Amida File:Tateshinayama-fuyu.JPG|{{ill|Mount Tateshina|en|Mount Tateshina|WD=Q717453|lt=Mount Tateshina|1=Mount Tateshina}} File:Yatsugatake-Kogen-Higashizawa-Bridge.jpg|Mt. Yatsugatake and Higashizawa Bridge {{ill|Hokuto, Yamanashi|en|Hokuto, Yamanashi|WD=Q825272|lt=Hokuto|1=Hokuto, Yamanashi}}, {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|en|Yamanashi Prefecture|WD=Q132720|lt=Yamanashi|1=Yamanashi Prefecture}} </gallery>

{{coord|35.9708|N|138.3700|E|source:wikidata|display=title}}
